questions.question_text: Under HAR 16-99-11, every real estate advertisement must include which information?
questions.explanation: Hawaii requires all real estate advertising to include the legal name of the brokerage (or registered trade name) and the license number. This applies across all media including internet and social media.

questions.question_text: A salesperson posts a property listing on social media using only their personal name. What is missing?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name and license number
questions.explanation: Hawaii advertising rules require the brokerage name (or trade name) and license number on all advertising, including social media posts. Individual licensees must also include their licensed name and designation.

questions.question_text: A team creates a flyer where the team name is displayed larger than the brokerage name. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, team names must not exceed brokerage size
distractors: Yes, team branding has no restrictions
Yes, if the broker approves it
Only if the team has 5 or more agents
questions.explanation: Hawaii rules require that team names not be displayed larger than the brokerage name or logo on any advertising materials. The principal broker is responsible for ensuring compliance.

questions.question_text: Who bears ultimate responsibility for advertising compliance at a brokerage?
questions.explanation: The principal broker is responsible for all advertising compliance at the brokerage. This includes ensuring all agents, teams, and the brokerage itself meet HAR 16-99-11 requirements across all media.

questions.question_text: An individual licensee's advertisement must include their licensed name plus which designation?
questions.explanation: Hawaii requires individual licensees to include their licensed name and their license designation: B (Broker), S (Salesperson), R (REALTOR), or RA (REALTOR Associate) in all advertising.

questions.question_text: Under HRS 467-1.5, does hiring a real estate salesperson automatically create an employer-employee relationship?
questions.correct_answer: No, IC status is also recognized
distractors: Yes, unless the broker opts out
Only if the salesperson requests it
questions.explanation: HRS 467-1.5 explicitly states that Hawaii law does not automatically create an employer-employee relationship. Both employment and independent contractor arrangements are recognized.

questions.question_text: A broker classifies an agent as an independent contractor. Which IRS safe harbor element is required?
questions.explanation: The IRS safe harbor for IC status requires that the agent be licensed, compensated by commission (not salary), and have a written independent contractor agreement. Fixed salaries and mandatory hours suggest employee status.

questions.question_text: A salesperson classified as an IC is paid solely by commission. Must they still work under a broker?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, broker supervision is always required
distractors: Only for the first year of practice
questions.explanation: Regardless of whether a salesperson is classified as an employee or independent contractor, Hawaii law requires all salespersons to work under the direction of a licensed broker.

questions.question_text: Which compensation method supports independent contractor classification under IRS rules?
questions.explanation: Under IRS safe harbor guidelines, independent contractors should be paid by commission rather than salary or hourly wages. Commission-based compensation is one of three key elements for IC classification.

questions.question_text: A broker provides set work hours and a salary to a salesperson with an IC agreement. What risk exists?
questions.correct_answer: IRS may reclassify as an employee
distractors: No risk if both parties agree
questions.explanation: Despite a written IC agreement, providing set work hours and salary contradicts IRS safe harbor requirements. The IRS may reclassify the salesperson as an employee, creating tax liability for the broker.

questions.question_text: Which licensee is responsible for maintaining brokerage policies and procedures?
questions.explanation: The principal broker is responsible for supervision, maintaining policies and procedures, and ensuring compliance with all real estate laws and rules at the brokerage.

questions.question_text: A salesperson at a brokerage violates advertising rules. Who may face disciplinary action from HIREC?
questions.explanation: The principal broker is responsible for all advertising compliance at the brokerage. When a salesperson violates advertising rules, both the salesperson and the principal broker may face discipline.

questions.question_text: A brokerage has 15 salespersons. Who must ensure all agents comply with HRS 467?
distractors: Each agent ensures their own compliance
questions.explanation: Regardless of brokerage size, the principal broker holds ultimate responsibility for supervising all agents and ensuring compliance with HRS 467 and HAR Chapter 99.

questions.question_text: A principal broker sends a mass email marketing campaign. What must the email contain?
questions.explanation: Hawaii advertising rules apply equally to all media including email. Every marketing communication must include the legal name of the brokerage (or registered trade name) and the license number.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed salesperson asks if the principal broker must review their transactions. What is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the PB must supervise all agents
questions.explanation: The principal broker must supervise all salespersons at the brokerage regardless of experience level or IC/employee status. This includes reviewing transactions and ensuring proper procedures are followed.
